result_raw: Russian victory – 1st clash Confederate victory – 2nd clash Russian victory – 3rd clash

commander_side_a_raw: RUS Alexander Suvorov RUS Ivan Drewitz
commander_side_b_raw: Kingdom of France Charles Dumouriez Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth Józef Miączyński Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth Kajetan Sapieha Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth Michał Walewski
strength_side_a_raw: 1,000 infantry*Władysław Konopczyński: According to a French officer, Suvorov had 1,800 men, of which 800 cavalry (it was uncommitted).Konopczyński 1928 *Władysław Miączyński: A report from the Russian side suggested that Suvorov's forces were much smaller and may have numbered as few as 150 soldiers.Miączyński 1911 *Alexander Petrushevsky: "His corps [Suvorov's] was not numerous; never afterward did it reach 4,000, and at the beginning it was even less." (1st & 2nd clashes)*3,000–3,500 men; *8 cannons.Petrov 1874 (3rd clash)
strength_side_b_raw: 700 cavalry & infantryAccording to Polish data, 400 cavalrymen & 80 infantrymen during the first engagement (no infantry involved).Konopczyński 1928 According to Russian data, 300 infantrymen in the second engagement. (1st & 2nd clashes) 3,500–4,000 men; *32 cannons, of which 2 deployed.Petrov 1874There were 30 cannons in the town and castle, which Dumouriez did not use. (3rd clash)
casualties_side_a_raw: Unknown (1st clash)GreatOsipov wrote of Suvorov's "great losses", without specifying the number.Osipov 1939 Along with the officers and Suvorov himself, who was wounded: 37 casualties according to Petrushevsky. A Polish source claimed that a French officer suggested Russian casualties of 400–500.Konopczyński 1928 (2nd clash)NegligiblePetrushevsky wrote that the loss of the Russians was negligible/insignificant, and was mostly done by gunshots fired from the Lanckorona Castle during the Russian pursuit of the retreating Poles, but no number is specified. The offence that decided the battle was carried out so quickly that the attackers were almost unharmed. (3rd clash)
casualties_side_b_raw: Unknown (1st clash)UncertainAccording to a French officer's account, the Poles were to lose 11 men killed.Konopczyński 1928 (2nd clash)*500 killedPetrov 1874Osipov 1939 and 200 prisoners;Petrov 1874 *2 cannons. (3rd clash)

date_raw: 37 or 36 BCJosephus simultaneously dates the siege and capture to when Agrippa and Gallus were consuls at Rome (37 BC), and to 27 years after Pompey's capture of the city in 63 (36 BC). Emil Schürer (1891) tried to reconcile the two conflicting statements by proposing that Josephus counted the years inclusively, placing the siege in 37, and a majority of scholars have concurred with this view. Filmer, Steinmann and others argued that Josephus's reckoning of dates by Roman consuls is unreliable and conflicts with external evidence, and that 36 should be preferred.FilmerSteinmann
